agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days. The platform serves B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. The engineering team frames this as a storage cost optimization with no business logic impact — this framing is dangerously incorrect.","bounded_context":"Delivery Records / Data Lifecycle Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete data artifact for a shipment lifecycle: sender, recipient, timestamps, proof of delivery, customs declarations, weight/dimensions, routing, and financial line items."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The rules governing how long delivery records are stored, driven by regulatory minimums, contractual obligations, and business needs — not storage convenience."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a shipment was delivered (signature, photo, GPS coordinates, timestamp). Legally required for dispute resolution and chargeback defense."},{"term":"B2B Compliance Reporting","definition":"The practice where business customers use platform delivery data to satisfy their own regulatory obligations (tax filings, customs audits, supply chain compliance)."},{"term":"Statutory Retention Period","definition":"The legally mandated minimum period for which certain business records must be preserved, varying by jurisdiction and record type."},{"term":"Right to Erasure","definition":"GDPR Article 17 right allowing data subjects to request deletion, which is overridden when retention is required for legal obligations (Article 17(3)(b))."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records must be retained for at least the minimum period required by applicable tax and customs regulations in each operating jurisdiction.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Violating statutory retention periods exposes the company to regulatory penalties, failed audits, and potential criminal liability. This is a legal constraint, not a policy choice.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery record in any jurisdiction WHEN the statutory retention period has not elapsed THEN the record MUST NOT be deleted or made inaccessible."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery records that serve as proof of delivery must be retained for at least the limitation period for contractual disputes in the applicable jurisdiction.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without POD data, the platform cannot defend against delivery dispute claims, chargebacks, or insurance claims. Destruction of evidence during limitation periods is legally indefensible.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery with associated POD WHEN a dispute or chargeback is filed within the limitation period THEN the full POD record MUST be retrievable."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"B2B customers must retain access to delivery data for the duration specified in their service agreements or required for their own compliance obligations.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Contractual obligations can be renegotiated, but unilateral deletion of data customers depend on for compliance reporting would constitute a breach of contract and a breach of trust.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a B2B customer with an active service agreement WHEN they request historical delivery data within the contractually agreed retention window THEN the data MUST be available."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Any data retention policy change must undergo a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) when it affects processing of personal data at scale in EU/UK jurisdictions.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"GDPR Article 35 requires DPIAs for changes to processing that are likely to result in high risk to data subjects. Bulk deletion of delivery records containing personal data qualifies.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a proposed retention policy change affecting personal data WHEN the change is evaluated THEN a completed DPIA must exist before implementation."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Delivery records containing customs declaration data for cross-border EU/UK shipments must be retained per customs regulations (typically 3-7 years depending on jurisdiction).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"EU Customs Code Article 51 requires retention of customs-relevant documents. Post-Brexit UK customs regulations impose similar requirements. 90-day deletion would destroy legally mandated records.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a cross-border delivery with customs declarations WHEN 90 days have elapsed THEN the record MUST still be retained if the jurisdiction requires longer retention."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A delivery record subject to EU VAT regulations (typically 6-7 year retention requirement)","when":"The auto-deletion job runs at 90 days","then":"The record MUST NOT be deleted; it must be retained for the full statutory period."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A B2B customer who uses delivery data for their annual compliance reporting","when":"They request delivery records from 6 months ago","then":"The records must be available and complete, not auto-deleted at 90 days."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A consumer files a dispute about a delivery that occurred 4 months ago","when":"Customer support or legal team attempts to retrieve the delivery record and POD","then":"The full record including POD must be retrievable to defend the claim."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A cross-border shipment with customs declarations","when":"A customs audit is initiated within the statutory retention period (up to 7 years)","then":"All customs-relevant delivery data must be producible for the audit."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"Any change to the data retention policy","when":"Before implementation begins","then":"A DPIA must be completed and approved, and legal counsel must confirm compliance with all applicable retention regulations."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"The platform operates in multiple EU/UK jurisdictions with different retention requirements","when":"Designing any new retention policy","then":"Retention periods must be jurisdiction-aware and apply the correct minimum per record type and country, not a single global TTL."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Cross-border shipment touching 3 jurisdictions with different retention requirements (e.g., France sender, Belgium transit, Germany recipient)","expected_behavior":"The longest applicable statutory retention period across all jurisdictions must apply to the record."},{"scenario":"B2B customer's contract specifies 5-year data availability but platform deletes at 90 days","expected_behavior":"This is a breach of contract. The platform must honor the contractual retention commitment regardless of internal storage policy."},{"scenario":"Active insurance claim or legal dispute on a delivery record that is past 90 days","expected_behavior":"Records under legal hold or involved in active disputes must never be auto-deleted regardless of any retention policy."},{"scenario":"Consumer exercises GDPR Right to Erasure on a delivery record that is also needed for tax compliance","expected_behavior":"Legal obligation exception (Article 17(3)(b)) overrides the erasure request. The record must be retained for the statutory period. The consumer must be informed of the legal basis for retention."},{"scenario":"Delivery data exported to B2B customer's system before 90-day deletion","expected_behavior":"Even if the customer has a copy, the platform remains the system of record. Deletion from the platform may invalidate the data's evidentiary value for audits."},{"scenario":"Historical analytics and trend data derived from delivery records","expected_behavior":"Aggregated/anonymized data may be retained, but if original records are needed to validate analytics or respond to regulatory inquiries, deletion at 90 days destroys the audit trail."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE. EU tax authorities can impose penalties of up to 100% of undeclared VAT for failure to produce records. Customs non-compliance fines range from thousands to millions of euros. B2B contract breach claims could include damages for customers' own compliance failures caused by missing data. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory penalty.","operational":"CRITICAL. Loss of delivery records after 90 days makes it impossible to resolve customer disputes, defend chargebacks, support insurance claims, or respond to regulatory audits. Customer support and legal teams lose access to historical data essential for daily operations. B2B customers who depend on the platform for compliance reporting would need to find alternative solutions or leave.","user":"HIGH. B2C consumers lose the ability to reference past deliveries, file late disputes, or request proof of delivery after 90 days. B2B customers face compliance gaps in their own reporting when platform data disappears. Trust in the platform as a reliable system of record is destroyed.","risk":"EXTREME. This change creates regulatory non-compliance across multiple frameworks simultaneously: EU VAT Directive, EU Customs Code, UK tax regulations, GDPR (ironically — deleting data you are legally required to retain is also a GDPR violation). Additionally, bulk deletion without a DPIA is itself a regulatory violation. The reputational and legal exposure dwarfs any storage cost savings.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"Loss of compliance data they depend on for tax filings, customs audits, and regulatory reporting. Could trigger their own regulatory violations. Likely contract breach."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"Loss of delivery history, inability to file disputes or request POD after 90 days. Degraded trust and customer experience."},{"group":"Finance / Tax Team","impact":"Inability to produce records for VAT audits. Direct regulatory penalty exposure."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance Team","impact":"Cannot defend disputes, chargebacks, or litigation. Destruction of evidence during limitation periods."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Cannot investigate or resolve historical delivery issues beyond 90 days."},{"group":"Data Protection Officer","impact":"Must conduct DPIA before any implementation. Responsible for ensuring GDPR compliance of the new policy."},{"group":"Engineering Team","impact":"Must implement jurisdiction-aware retention (not a simple global TTL), legal holds, and tiered storage — significantly more complex than proposed."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The specification is missing critical information that must be provided before any analysis can be conclusive: specific regulatory retention requirements per jurisdiction, existing B2B contractual terms, legal counsel review, and DPIA results. The 90-day proposal as stated is almost certainly non-compliant, but the exact compliant retention periods depend on jurisdiction-specific legal analysis that was never provided.","high_confidence_areas":["90 days is far below statutory minimums for EU/UK tax and customs records","B2B customers will be materially harmed by loss of compliance data","A DPIA is required before implementation","This is NOT merely an infrastructure optimization — it changes business capabilities"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact statutory retention periods per jurisdiction and record type — requires legal counsel input","Specific B2B contractual retention commitments — requires contract review","Whether tiered storage (hot/cold/archive) was considered as an alternative","Whether the platform has any existing legal holds process"],"assumptions":["The platform processes VAT-relevant transactions in EU jurisdictions","Cross-border shipments include customs declarations","B2B contracts include data availability provisions","No legal counsel was consulted before this proposal was made"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"No legal or regulatory analysis was provided for this proposal. EU VAT Directive requires 6-7 year retention for transaction records. EU Customs Code Article 51 requires multi-year retention for customs documents. UK tax regulations require 6-year retention. None of these statutory requirements were referenced or analyzed. A 90-day retention policy cannot be evaluated without jurisdiction-specific legal review.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance Team","Finance/Tax Team","B2B Customers","Data Protection Officer"],"options":["Halt proposal until legal counsel provides jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction retention requirements","Engage DPO to initiate a DPIA before any design work proceeds","Commission a regulatory gap analysis comparing 90-day policy against all applicable retention mandates"],"recommendation":"STOP. Do not proceed with implementation. This proposal requires legal review, a DPIA, and B2B contract analysis before any technical design begins. The framing as 'no business logic changes' is incorrect — this is a compliance-critical change. If the goal is cost reduction, evaluate tiered storage (hot/warm/cold/archive) that preserves data for statutory periods while reducing active storage costs."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"B2B customer contractual terms regarding data retention and availability were not provided. If contracts guarantee multi-year data access, unilateral deletion at 90 days constitutes breach of contract. The scope of contractual exposure is unknown without contract review.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","Legal/Compliance Team","Sales/Account Management"],"options":["Review all active B2B contracts for data retention clauses","Survey B2B customers on their retention dependencies","Grandfather existing contracts while negotiating new terms"],"recommendation":"Conduct a full B2B contract audit to identify retention obligations before designing any new policy."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) has been performed or referenced. Under GDPR Article 35, large-scale changes to personal data processing require a DPIA. Bulk auto-deletion of delivery records containing personal data across EU/UK markets qualifies.","affected_stakeholders":["Data Protection Officer","Legal/Compliance Team","All data subjects (B2C and B2B contacts)"],"options":["Initiate DPIA immediately as a prerequisite to any design work","Consult with the DPO on whether the change triggers Article 35 thresholds"],"recommendation":"A DPIA must be completed and documented before any retention policy change is implemented. This is not optional in EU/UK jurisdictions."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Proposed 90-day retention period falls below statutory minimum retention requirements for tax and customs records in EU/UK jurisdictions.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1"},{"violation":"Auto-deletion at 90 days would destroy proof of delivery needed to defend disputes and chargebacks within the legal limitation period (typically 2-6 years).","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"},{"violation":"Unilateral deletion of delivery data likely breaches B2B customer contracts that depend on historical data for compliance reporting.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"},{"violation":"No DPIA has been conducted for this change, which is mandatory under GDPR Article 35 before implementation.","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"},{"violation":"Cross-border customs declaration data would be destroyed well before the statutory retention period expires.","business_rule_broken":"BR-5"},{"violation":"The proposal is framed as 'no business logic changes' which is factually incorrect — it eliminates the platform's ability to serve as a system of record, defend disputes, support audits, and fulfill contractual obligations.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, BR-3"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days. The platform serves B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. The engineering team frames this as a storage cost optimization with no business logic impact — this framing is dangerously incorrect.","bounded_context":"Delivery Records / Data Lifecycle Management","ubiquitous_

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this carefully. This is a data retention policy change for delivery records in a logistics/courier platform operating in EU and UK markets. The engineering team frames it as "infrastructure optimization" but this has massive domain implications.

Key concerns:
1. GDPR requires data retention policies, but other regulations require MINIMUM retention periods
2. EU/UK customs, tax, and VAT regulations typically require 6-7 year retention
3. B2B customers depend on this data for compliance reporting
4. 90 days is dangerously short for logistics records

This is a missing_context situation — no, actually, the engineering team stated requirements clearly but they conflict with regulatory reality. 

Wait — I'm realizing the real problem here. The engineering team never consulted legal or regulatory experts, and nobody in this scenario actually provided the regulatory requirements that would govern this decision. The team's claim that there are "no business logic changes" misses the point entirely — this isn't about whether the code works, it's about whether the policy itself is legally compliant. That's the missing context: nobody analyzed what retention periods are actually mandated by law.
